title: Design retrieval and spacing for durable performance

objectives.statement: Distinguish familiarity-producing study activity from retrieval that supplies evidence about durable performance.
Construct and justify a retrieval-and-spacing plan from a diagnostic state, target performance, and retention horizon.
Interpret performance across attempts and adapt support, spacing, or task mixture without declaring mastery from one fluent encounter.

scenes.title: From feeling familiar to performing later
scenes.contentHtml:
From feeling familiar to performing later

You read an explanation twice. The second reading is smooth: every sentence seems recognizable, and the worked steps are easy to follow. Two days later, with the page closed, you cannot reconstruct the central relation. Which moment should guide your study plan—the smooth rereading or the later attempt?

This lesson builds a plan around the later operation. You will define what you must be able to do, attempt that performance from memory, use feedback to repair the attempt, and schedule changed retrieval opportunities across time. The result is not a magic calendar. It is an evidence loop in which observed performance changes the next study action.

By the end, you will be able to:

  1. distinguish activity that produces familiarity from retrieval that tests a declared performance;
  2. construct a retrieval-and-spacing plan from a diagnostic baseline and a retention horizon; and
  3. adapt support, timing, and task mixture from a sequence of attempts without declaring mastery from one success.

In this lesson, mastery is a provisional, bounded judgment: the learner has repeatedly reconstructed or used the target knowledge without the source, after delay and with relevant variation. That operational definition is deliberately stricter than “I recognize this” and deliberately narrower than a claim of permanent knowledge or professional competence.

Callout
label Scope and exclusions

The lesson teaches academic study-plan design. It does not supply a universally optimal spacing interval, diagnose memory ability, guarantee an examination result, award university credit, or establish clinical competence. The fictional study cases contain no medical or personal data.

Source Note
claims claim-mastery-performance, claim-retrieval-durabilitysources source-authoring-evidence, source-content-standard, source-roediger-karpicke-2006, source-adesope-et-al-2017

The repository synthesis, a primary prose experiment, and a broader practice-testing meta-analysis support retrieval for later retention across varied educational conditions and warn that familiarity and immediate fluency are not durable-learning evidence. The project-internal standard turns that evidence into the bounded performance model used here; it is a design convention rather than independent empirical support. Effects and useful task forms still vary by learner, material, feedback, and final assessment.

scenes.title: Activate your diagnostic baseline
scenes.contentHtml:
Activate your diagnostic baseline

Use one target from your prior topic-orientation-diagnostic-baseline work. Do not reopen the source yet.

  1. Name the target knowledge in one line.
  2. State the future performance you care about: for example, explain a relation, select a model, calculate a result, interpret data, or construct a representation.
  3. Mark your most recent unaided evidence as unfamiliar, fragile, or secure.
  4. Write the observation behind that label. Record what you produced, what prompts or notes were available, when the attempt occurred, and which part failed or succeeded.

Do not upgrade a classification because the page looks recognizable now. Preserve the earlier observation so the new plan has a genuine starting state.

Route decision

Continue if you can state both a target performance and a recent unaided observation. Keep your classification available for the worked example.

Recovery route

If you cannot state a target performance or have no unaided observation, return to topic-orientation-diagnostic-baseline (PREM-01.02). Complete one low-stakes closed-source attempt, compare it with the relevant criteria, and record the result. Then restart this scene. The route supplies missing planning information; it is not a penalty or a grade.

If the word secure appears in your baseline but the record contains only rereading, copying, or following a displayed solution, leave the label undecided and collect an unaided attempt before continuing.

Source Note
claims claim-mastery-performancesources source-authoring-evidence, source-content-standard

The instruction to withhold a mastery classification from familiarity or displayed performance alone applies the lesson's bounded mastery-evidence model. The sources support judging later unaided performance rather than exposure fluency; they do not establish one universal mastery threshold.

scenes.title: A design loop for durable performance
scenes.contentHtml:
A design loop for durable performance

A retrieval-and-spacing plan is a chain of decisions, not a list of dates. Each opportunity must identify an operation, evidence criteria, a response to error, and a reason for its position in the sequence.

Definition
id definition-target-performancelabel Target performance

A target performance states what the learner will generate or do, without which supports, in what kind of context, and by what retention horizon. “Study membrane transport” names content; “compare two transport models without notes and justify the selection for a new case in twelve days” names a performance.

Definition
id definition-retrieval-practicelabel Retrieval practice

Retrieval practice is an attempt to generate a relevant answer, explanation, prediction, representation, or procedure from memory before consulting the answer source. Copying a visible answer is production, but it is not retrieval because the source supplies the content during the attempt.

Definition
id definition-spacinglabel Spacing and retention horizon

Spacing distributes learning or retrieval opportunities across time. A retention horizon is the whole future period over which the learner needs to retain and use the target knowledge. It constrains the plan. In the cited spacing literature, the technical retention interval is narrower: it is the time from the last practice opportunity to the later test. Neither term supplies one best gap for every learner and task.

The six-part loop

1. Specify the operation

Convert a topic label into observable performance. Name the conditions that matter. If the eventual task requires selection among models, recognition of model names is insufficient. If the eventual task requires an explanation, a list of isolated terms is insufficient.

2. Retrieve before review

Attempt the operation with the planned supports absent. The attempt may be short, but it must expose the reasoning you need to judge. A prompt such as “write every fact you remember” can miss the target if the goal is to select a model and justify it.

3. Compare with criteria

Use an answer model, rubric, or verified source after the attempt. Mark the first missing or incorrect relation, not only a total score. The location of the error makes the next action more specific.

4. Recover deliberately

Choose a recovery action that addresses the error: rebuild a missing prerequisite, compare two boundary cases, study one worked model, or correct an operation. Then retrieve again; rereading alone does not show whether the repair can be generated.

5. Retrieve after delay and variation

Place another opportunity after some time has passed, and change a feature that matters to the target performance. Variation might change the case, require a different representation, or remove a prompt. A changed number with identical reasoning is near practice; it does not by itself test adaptable use.

6. Apply a decision rule

A decision rule links an observation to the next step. For example: “If an essential relation is missing, use a focused worked model and retry a related case sooner. If the relation is reconstructed accurately after delay in a changed case, reduce support and place a cumulative check closer to the retention horizon.” This is an author-created planning heuristic, not a universal scheduling law.

Block first when acquisition is incomplete; mix when selection is the goal

Interleaving mixes already introduced categories or strategies so the learner must choose among them. It can serve a discrimination goal when each category has a usable initial representation. If the learner is still acquiring the first procedure, a complete worked model and initially blocked practice may be the more coherent starting point. The design question is therefore “What operation must improve now?” rather than “Is mixed practice always better?”

One representation, two reading routes

The table compresses the loop for auditing. The numbered sections above are its linear, nonvisual equivalent.

Plan field Question it answers Evidence recorded
Target performance What must be generated, under which conditions, by when? Explicit operation and criteria
Baseline What happened on the most recent unaided attempt? Error pattern, support, and timing
Retrieval task What will the learner generate before review? Learner response
Feedback and recovery Which relation failed, why, and what happens next? Criterion comparison and repair action
Spacing and variation When and in what changed form will retrieval recur? Later attempt under stated conditions
Decision rule How will success or error alter the next opportunity? A predeclared adaptation
Source Note
claims claim-mastery-performance, claim-retrieval-durability, claim-spacing-retention-horizon, claim-feedback-recovery, claim-interleaving-conditionalsources source-authoring-evidence, source-content-standard, source-roediger-karpicke-2006, source-adesope-et-al-2017, source-cepeda-et-al-2006, source-wisniewski-et-al-2020, source-brunmair-richter-2019

The external primary and meta-analytic records support retrieval before answer reveal, spacing across opportunities, information-rich feedback, and conditional interleaving within their recorded populations and task limits. The repository synthesis integrates those heterogeneous findings. The six-part loop is an original instructional synthesis aligned to the project-internal standard, not a validated universal algorithm.

scenes.title: Build a fourteen-day retrieval plan
scenes.contentHtml:
Build a fourteen-day retrieval plan

The following case is fictional and teaches planning, not biology. Its day gaps illustrate one defensible schedule; they are not claimed to be optimal.

Worked Example
id worked-example-fourteen-day-planlabel From fragile baseline to changed-case performance

Task. Ari has fourteen days before a seminar. The seminar requires an unaided explanation of a previously taught scientific mechanism and selection of that mechanism for a new bounded case. Ari's baseline is fragile: immediately after study, Ari can name the five parts but omits two causal links when explaining why one change leads to the next.

Represent the givens, constraints, and unknowns.

  • Givens: fourteen-day horizon; five-part mechanism already introduced; two causal links missing; future task includes explanation and selection for a new case.
  • Constraints: attempts must be unaided before feedback; the plan must fit ordinary individual study; no one spacing interval is assumed universally best.
  • Unknowns: whether the missing links can be reconstructed after recovery, whether the mechanism can be selected for a changed case, and how much support later attempts require.

Select the model. The six-part retrieval loop fits because the goal is later generation and selection, not simply exposure. Ari needs a complete initial model of the planning procedure, then decreasing support and changed cases.

Plan. Define criteria, retrieve now, diagnose and repair the two missing links, then collect evidence across separated and varied attempts. Use each observation to choose the next task.

Opportunity Operation before review Feedback and recovery Decision rule
Day 0 Write the five-part mechanism as an ordered causal explanation; underline each “because” relation. Compare against five part criteria and four link criteria. Study one worked explanation only for each link missing in this new attempt, then close it and reconstruct the chain once. If any essential link remains absent, repeat a related supported retrieval before increasing the gap.
Day 2 Reconstruct the mechanism as a labelled relation list rather than the original prose. Check whether every causal link survives the representation change. Explain aloud or in writing why each link is needed. If the chain is correct only in the original wording, vary representation again before adding model-selection practice.
Day 6 Given two already introduced mechanisms and a new case, select one and justify the selection from its conditions. Compare the justification with the selection criteria; target any confused boundary with a contrasting pair. If selection conditions are confused, return briefly to blocked case comparison and schedule another changed case sooner.
Day 10 Explain the mechanism to a fictional novice in six sentences, including one boundary where it would not apply. Use the same causal and boundary criteria; replace vague labels with the specific relation they refer to. If the explanation is accurate after delay and variation, remove remaining prompts and retain the final cumulative check.
Day 13 Complete an unaided changed-case explanation and selection under the seminar's expected format. Compare with the full rubric and record the first error, not just a score. If accurate, carry one later cumulative retrieval into the course. If not, repair the exact relation and renew retrieval; do not hide the error by rereading repeatedly.

Execute the first decision. Ari's fresh day-0 attempt includes all five parts and recovers one of the two links that were missing at baseline, but it still misses the relation between parts two and three. The new attempt therefore replaces the baseline error count as the evidence governing the next action. Ari does not label the whole mechanism “almost mastered”; Ari studies a worked account of the one link still missing, explains why the link is necessary, closes the source, and reconstructs the chain.

Check the plan. Every opportunity names an unaided operation. Feedback follows rather than precedes the attempt. Later tasks vary representation, selection, audience, or case. The final opportunity resembles the required performance without copying the original example. The plan leaves room to change after errors.

Interpretation. One successful reconstruction on day 0 would show that the immediate repair can be generated. It would not show that Ari can still explain and select the mechanism later. Evidence becomes more relevant to the seminar goal as performance survives delay, reduced support, and a changed case.

Self-explanation prompt. Which single design decision prevents Ari's day-0 success from being mistaken for mastery? State why.

The key decision is to predeclare later changed retrieval opportunities and judge those performances. The exact numbered days are replaceable. What must remain is the alignment among target performance, observed errors, feedback, delayed variation, and the next-step rule.

For a non-tabular route, read each row as four sentences: on the named day, Ari retrieves before review; compares the response with criteria; repairs a specific gap; then lets that result determine the next opportunity. Nothing depends on the table's position or visual layout.

Source Note
claims claim-mastery-performance, claim-retrieval-durability, claim-spacing-retention-horizon, claim-feedback-recovery, claim-guidance-fadingsources source-authoring-evidence, source-content-standard, source-roediger-karpicke-2006, source-adesope-et-al-2017, source-cepeda-et-al-2006, source-wisniewski-et-al-2020, source-barbieri-et-al-2023, source-tetzlaff-et-al-2025

Permitted worked-example evidence supports a complete model during early mathematics learning, while expertise-reversal evidence supports adjusting assistance in light of demonstrated prior knowledge and performance. The progression used here—from a complete model through completion, supported, independent, mixed-selection, and transfer tasks—is an EmbeddedKnowledge-authored implementation of those bounded principles, not an empirically fixed ladder. Retrieval, feedback, delay, and changed cases have their separately listed support. The schedule, case, criteria, and decision rules are original teaching constructions and are not empirical claims that these exact days optimize learning.

scenes.title: Reconstruct the design loop
scenes.contentHtml:
Reconstruct the design loop

Keep the concept scene closed. Write brief answers before moving to the feedback section.

Check
id check-reconstruct-looplabel Low-stakes retrieval before feedback
  1. Name four components that turn a calendar entry into an interpretable retrieval opportunity.
  2. Explain why a correct answer immediately after rereading does not by itself support a durable-mastery judgment.
  3. A delayed attempt contains the correct conclusion but omits the governing relation. What should the feedback and next step do?

Feedback

  1. A complete answer can name the target operation, attempt conditions, criteria, feedback and recovery action, spacing and variation, or a performance-linked decision rule. Any four are acceptable if they form a usable chain. If you named only dates, return to the planning table and add what the learner will generate and how the result changes the next step.
  2. The attempt is too close to exposure and does not test whether the operation can be reconstructed after delay or variation. If you wrote only “memory gets worse,” make the reasoning more precise: the design needs evidence under conditions that resemble the future demand.
  3. Feedback should identify the missing relation, explain why it governs the conclusion, and prescribe a targeted recovery followed by renewed retrieval. If your next step is “read everything again,” narrow it to the failed relation and then test whether the repair can be generated.

Now retrieve the six-part loop in your own order. A defensible sequence is: specify performance, retrieve, compare, recover, retrieve after delay and variation, then apply a decision rule. Different wording is acceptable when every relation remains explicit.

Source Note
claims claim-mastery-performance, claim-retrieval-durability, claim-feedback-recoverysources source-authoring-evidence, source-content-standard, source-roediger-karpicke-2006, source-adesope-et-al-2017, source-wisniewski-et-al-2020

The prompt-before-feedback order and corrective response follow the bounded retrieval and feedback evidence summarized by the repository. The project-internal standard supplies the lesson's design convention, not a separate empirical warrant. The check is low stakes and supplies practice evidence only; completing it does not by itself establish mastery.

scenes.title: Repair the fluency-equals-mastery model
scenes.contentHtml:
Repair the fluency-equals-mastery model

First commit to a prediction: after two smooth rereadings, Lee can explain every displayed step while the page is open. Lee has not attempted the explanation without the page. Should Lee mark the target secure and move on? Write yes or no, then give one reason.

Misconception
id misconception-fluency-equals-masterylabel “If it feels easy now, I have mastered it”

The plausible model. Ease while reading seems informative because effort has fallen and each sentence is recognizable. The model infers that lower effort during exposure means the knowledge will be available later.

What the model cannot explain. Repeated exposure can produce strong immediate fluency while a later closed-source attempt remains incomplete. The open page supplies cues and relations that the later task may withhold. Therefore the observation and the intended conclusion occur under different conditions.

Replacement model. Treat fluency as an experience during study, not as sufficient mastery evidence. Define the future operation, attempt it without the answer source, compare the generated response with criteria, repair the first material error, and collect another attempt after delay or meaningful variation.

Compare on the same case. Under the fluency model, Lee marks the target secure because reading is smooth. Under the performance model, Lee records “evidence missing,” closes the page, attempts the explanation, and lets the observed causal omissions determine recovery.

Recheck in a new case. Morgan watches a complete worked solution and can predict each visible next line. The final task will require selecting and executing the method without prompts in six days. What evidence is missing? A strong answer says that Morgan has not yet selected and executed the method unaided, either now or after delay. The next action is an unaided attempt followed by criterion-based feedback, not a confidence rating alone.

If your opening prediction was yes, do not erase it. Write the observation that changed the inference: the open source supplies cues that the future closed-source performance withholds. Then schedule one attempt that tests the intended operation.

If your prediction was no but your reason was only “rereading is bad,” refine it. Rereading can be a recovery action when it targets a diagnosed gap. The error is using exposure fluency as sufficient evidence, not the mere presence of rereading in a plan.

Source Note
claims claim-mastery-performance, claim-retrieval-durability, claim-feedback-recoverysources source-authoring-evidence, source-content-standard, source-roediger-karpicke-2006, source-adesope-et-al-2017, source-wisniewski-et-al-2020

The repository synthesis reports that repeated study can look favorable on immediate performance while retrieval supports later retention under studied conditions. The repair therefore compares evidence conditions rather than banning rereading. Feedback and a renewed attempt remain necessary because retrieval without correction can leave an error intact.

scenes.title: Fade support and adapt the plan
scenes.contentHtml:
Fade support and adapt the plan

Complete each task before reading its feedback. Support decreases across the sequence. Use your own paper, plain text, speech-to-text, or another text-producing route; the reasoning standard is the same.

Completion task

A plan currently reads:

  • Target: explain three causal links without notes in eight days.
  • Baseline: fragile; link two is omitted.
  • Day 0: reconstruct the explanation, compare with three link criteria, study a worked account of link two, and reconstruct again.
  • Day 3: reconstruct the same wording.
  • Decision rule: if all links appear, mark mastered.

Replace the day-3 line and decision rule so the plan tests more than immediate repair or repeated wording.

Feedback

A strong completion changes the representation or case on day 3 and keeps mastery provisional. For example: “Day 3: explain the three links from a new starting condition. If link two fails, compare contrasting cases and retry sooner; if all links survive the changed case, remove prompts and schedule another cumulative attempt nearer day 8.” If you changed only the date, add a changed operation and a response to error.

Supported task

Sam is unfamiliar with two calculation procedures and cannot yet complete either worked model. The eventual assessment will mix the procedures. Choose the better immediate action and justify it:

  1. mix unsignalled problems from both procedures now; or
  2. study and complete a guided model for each procedure before later mixed selection.

Use this sentence stem if needed: “The immediate operation should be ___ because ___. Interleaving becomes useful when ___.”

Feedback

Option 2 better fits the stated baseline. Sam first needs a usable representation of each procedure. Later mixed problems can require selection once both procedures have initial support. If you selected option 1, check whether you treated interleaving as universally beneficial rather than matching it to the discrimination operation.

Independent task

Design a seven-day plan for this fictional case:

Noor must interpret a small graph and justify which of two already taught explanations fits. The baseline is fragile: Noor reads values accurately but selects an explanation from one memorable word rather than the relation shown by the graph.

Your plan must include:

  • the target performance and criteria;
  • at least three retrieval opportunities;
  • feedback and recovery for the word-cue error;
  • one meaningful change in graph or context;
  • a stated retention horizon; and
  • a decision rule after success and after error.

Feedback checklist

Use these questions rather than comparing exact dates:

  1. Does every opportunity require Noor to interpret and justify before seeing feedback?
  2. Does recovery contrast the misleading word cue with the graph relation that should govern selection?
  3. Does a later opportunity change the surface cue while preserving the selection demand?
  4. Does error trigger targeted support and renewed retrieval?
  5. Does successful delayed performance reduce support or extend to a cumulative check without becoming a claim of permanent mastery?

If any answer is no, revise the corresponding plan line. If all are yes, compare your schedule with the seven-day horizon and ensure the final attempt occurs under conditions relevant to the intended performance.

Exemplar after your attempt

One defensible fragment is: “Day 0: Noor closes the source, interprets the graph relation, selects an explanation, and justifies the selection before checking the criteria. If the memorable word still controls the choice, compare two graphs that share that word but show different relations, then retrieve the governing relation again. Day 2: select between the same explanations for an unseen graph with changed labels. Another word-cue error triggers one supported contrasting case and an earlier renewed attempt; accurate relation-based selection removes that support and leads to a changed-context check nearer day 7.” The dates are replaceable. The important feature is that the observed word-cue error changes the recovery, the next retrieval task, and the amount of support.

Mixed selection

For each action, label its primary job retrieval, feedback, recovery, or spacing. One action can contribute to more than one job, but choose the job that is decisive in the sentence.

  1. Close the source and explain the boundary condition.
  2. Compare the response against a rubric and identify the first missing relation.
  3. Rebuild that relation with one contrasting worked case.
  4. Schedule a changed-case attempt after a gap.

The intended primary labels are retrieval, feedback, recovery, and spacing, in that order. If two labels seemed interchangeable, state what observation would separate them. For example, a date supplies spacing only when a retrieval operation is actually scheduled on that date.

Source Note
claims claim-mastery-performance, claim-retrieval-durability, claim-spacing-retention-horizon, claim-feedback-recovery, claim-interleaving-conditional, claim-guidance-fadingsources source-authoring-evidence, source-content-standard, source-roediger-karpicke-2006, source-adesope-et-al-2017, source-cepeda-et-al-2006, source-wisniewski-et-al-2020, source-brunmair-richter-2019, source-barbieri-et-al-2023, source-tetzlaff-et-al-2025

Permitted evidence supports worked examples during early mathematics learning and adjusting assistance when prior knowledge or demonstrated performance changes. Retrieval, feedback, spacing, and conditional interleaving have their separately listed support. The exact sequence used here—completion, supported work, independent design, mixed selection, and transfer—is a project-authored progression for implementing those bounded principles; the cited research does not establish it as a fixed ladder. Exact plans remain learner- and task-dependent rather than universal prescriptions.

scenes.title: Redesign a peer study session
scenes.contentHtml:
Redesign a peer study session

Four learners have forty minutes together. Each has classified three targets at baseline, but their fragile targets differ. They need to use the ideas independently in a seminar nine days later. Their old routine was to reread one shared chapter aloud.

The group setting changes the design problem. A shared explanation can be useful feedback, yet it can also conceal who generated which relation. Your plan must preserve individual evidence while using the group efficiently.

Transfer task

Create a two-part plan: the forty-minute meeting and at least one later individual opportunity. Include all of the following:

  1. how each learner selects a target from the baseline;
  2. an individual retrieval operation before group feedback;
  3. a criterion-based comparison that does not become answer copying;
  4. targeted recovery for different error patterns;
  5. a conditional use of mixed selection only where the seminar requires discrimination among already introduced ideas;
  6. a later changed-context retrieval before day 9; and
  7. a decision rule for a missing core relation, a correct but case-bound response, and a correct delayed response.

Do not solve by giving every learner the same topic or by asking the most confident learner to lecture for forty minutes. Those choices remove the baseline differences that make adaptation necessary.

Model response after your attempt

One defensible plan uses four phases:

  • Minutes 0–4: each learner selects one fragile target and writes the expected performance and criteria.
  • Minutes 4–12: each learner completes a closed-source response individually. No peer answer is visible yet.
  • Minutes 12–28: learners exchange responses and use the criteria to mark the first missing or unsupported relation. Each learner states a specific recovery action. Peers ask for the governing relation instead of supplying a polished answer to copy.
  • Minutes 28–40: learners complete a short changed case or mixed-selection prompt only if the relevant categories are already introduced. Each learner then records an individual retrieval opportunity before day 9 in a different case or representation.

The decision rule is learner-specific. A missing core relation triggers a focused worked model and an earlier renewed retrieval. A correct response tied only to the original wording triggers a changed representation or case. A correct delayed response permits less support and a later cumulative check. None of the three observations fixes one universally correct next date.

Why this is transfer

The worked example planned for one fictional learner over fourteen days. This task coordinates four different baselines, scarce shared time, peer feedback, and a nine-day horizon. The retrieval loop remains, but its allocation and safeguards must change. Merely replacing Ari's name with four names would not solve the coordination problem.

Source Note
claims claim-mastery-performance, claim-retrieval-durability, claim-spacing-retention-horizon, claim-feedback-recovery, claim-interleaving-conditional, claim-guidance-fadingsources source-authoring-evidence, source-content-standard, source-roediger-karpicke-2006, source-adesope-et-al-2017, source-cepeda-et-al-2006, source-wisniewski-et-al-2020, source-brunmair-richter-2019, source-barbieri-et-al-2023, source-tetzlaff-et-al-2025

The transfer design preserves retrieval before feedback, delayed variation, performance-linked recovery, assistance adjusted to observed performance, and conditional interleaving. The permitted external records support those general principles only within their recorded task and population limits; the project standard supplies the authored implementation conventions. The group schedule and decision rules are original examples and do not assert that this minute-by-minute allocation is empirically optimal.

scenes.title: Demonstrate plan design and adaptation
scenes.contentHtml:
Demonstrate plan design and adaptation

Complete the four items in assessment.json without using the concept scene. You may write, type, dictate to speech-to-text, or use another text route. No timer is required, and presentation style is not scored.

The assessment collects twenty points:

  1. distinguish retrieval evidence from exposure activity: 2 points;
  2. design a twelve-day plan from a fragile baseline: 6 points;
  3. interpret and adapt a three-attempt record: 6 points; and
  4. transfer the loop to a constrained laboratory partnership: 6 points.

For this lesson's local mastery gate, a provisional success is at least 16 of 20 points, including at least 4 of 6 on plan design, at least 4 of 6 on interpretation and adaptation, and at least 4 of 6 on transfer. This threshold is an author-created assessment convention, not a universal psychometric definition of mastery. The criterion exists to prevent a correct recognition item or strength in one constructed response from compensating for an inability to construct, interpret, adapt, or transfer a plan.

Answer and feedback conditions

Attempt all items before consulting answer logic. After submission, compare each response with the analytic criteria, not only the total. For every missed criterion, write:

  • what the response did;
  • which planning relation is absent or incorrect; and
  • the next retrieval or recovery action.

Retry strategy

Retakes remain available. A retry must change the target topic, baseline error pattern, time horizon, or collaboration constraint so that the learner reasons again instead of replaying a memorized answer. Preserve the same outcome and scoring criteria.

Remediation routes

  • If item 1 is missed, return to the fluency-equals-mastery repair and distinguish visible production, recognition, and unaided generation.
  • If item 2 scores below 4, return to the planning table and ensure every date names an operation, feedback, recovery, variation, and decision rule.
  • If item 3 scores below 4, annotate each observation with “supports” and “does not support,” then redesign only the next opportunity.
  • If item 4 scores below 4, return to the transfer scene and protect individual retrieval before group feedback.

After remediation, use the equivalent retry form. Do not infer durable mastery from the immediate correction alone; place a later changed task into the next course lesson or study sequence.

Source Note
claims claim-mastery-performance, claim-retrieval-durability, claim-spacing-retention-horizon, claim-feedback-recovery, claim-interleaving-conditional, claim-guidance-fadingsources source-authoring-evidence, source-content-standard, source-roediger-karpicke-2006, source-adesope-et-al-2017, source-cepeda-et-al-2006, source-wisniewski-et-al-2020, source-brunmair-richter-2019, source-barbieri-et-al-2023, source-tetzlaff-et-al-2025

The assessment samples discrimination, design, interpretation, and transfer rather than treating one familiar response as mastery. Its retrieval, feedback, delayed practice, worked-model support, and performance-sensitive assistance are grounded in the bounded permitted evidence; their particular sequencing, retry form, and remediation routes follow the project-authored standard. The local point threshold is explicitly a lesson convention and awaits independent review; it is not a research-derived universal cutoff.

scenes.title: Carry the loop into later lessons
scenes.contentHtml:
Carry the loop into later lessons

You can now compress the design into one auditable statement:

Define the future performance, retrieve before review, compare against criteria, recover the first material error, retrieve again after delay and meaningful variation, and let observed performance change the next opportunity.

The statement is useful only if each verb names an action in the plan. “Review on Thursday” is incomplete. “On Thursday, close the source, select a model for a changed case, justify the selection against three criteria, then repair and reschedule from the observed error” is inspectable.

A final design audit

Before using a plan, ask:

  1. Alignment: Does the retrieval operation match what must be done later?
  2. Evidence: Does the attempt occur before answer review, and are criteria explicit?
  3. Recovery: Does feedback locate a relation and prescribe a next action?
  4. Spacing: Are opportunities distributed across the relevant horizon without claiming a universal gap?
  5. Variation: Does a later task change a case, representation, support, or selection demand that matters?
  6. Adaptation: Do success and error lead to different next steps?
  7. Boundary: Is interleaving reserved for discrimination among usable categories or strategies?
  8. Inference: Does the mastery judgment remain bounded by the task, delay, and conditions actually observed?

Delayed and cumulative retrieval links

Use this loop again rather than ending it here:

  • Within the next later lesson, retrieve the six-part loop before opening this scene.
  • Apply it to one domain-specific target chosen from your diagnostic baseline.
  • After a longer delay, compare the original plan with the attempt record and revise the decision rule.
  • Once several models or procedures have been introduced, add mixed selection only when choosing among them is part of the target performance.

The prerequisite also remains active: a plan starts from the topic-orientation-diagnostic-baseline observation rather than from a confidence label alone.

Accessibility and alternatives

Every required relationship appears in ordinary text and source order. The tables in earlier scenes repeat relationships that are also expressed as ordered lists and paragraphs. No distinction depends on color, location, motion, sound, hover, dragging, or time pressure. Learners may answer by keyboard, handwriting, speech-to-text, or another text route without changing the planning operation or rubric. At 200% zoom, narrow-screen reflow, reduced-motion settings, and print, follow the headings and lists in order; no hidden interaction contains essential content.

Educational boundary

The loop helps design and interpret academic practice. A successful lesson assessment does not certify permanent retention, university credit, admissions readiness, or professional or clinical competence. Only later performance under the relevant conditions can add evidence for the corresponding bounded claim.

Source Note
claims claim-mastery-performance, claim-retrieval-durability, claim-spacing-retention-horizon, claim-feedback-recovery, claim-interleaving-conditionalsources source-authoring-evidence, source-content-standard, source-roediger-karpicke-2006, source-adesope-et-al-2017, source-cepeda-et-al-2006, source-wisniewski-et-al-2020, source-brunmair-richter-2019

The synthesis preserves the evidence-supported principles and their limits: retrieval rather than exposure alone, distributed later opportunities, information-rich recovery, conditional mixture, and performance-sensitive adaptation. The external studies and reviews span heterogeneous learners, materials, comparisons, and outcomes, so neither they nor the repository synthesis justify one optimal schedule or a universal mastery threshold. The audit sequence itself is a project-authored implementation.

aiAssistance.disclosure: This lesson pack was revised by Codex CLI 0.144.6 using OpenAI GPT-5 in isolated author run /root/author-loop under accountable principal github:VSBDev. Version 0.1.4 responds to the preserved academic and learning-design approvals and accessibility-and-rights request-changes review of candidate 54f10aa5d4e3d0d226426dd3a70b611ad0572dc1 without performing a review or adjudication role. The recorded SHA-256 digest covers the exact discloseable author-revision assignment supplied to this run and excludes hidden provider and platform instructions. Earlier authoring digests and external feedback identifiers are preserved in REVISION-HISTORY.md.

assessment.title: Retrieval and spacing design provisional mastery gate

items.prompt: A learner must explain a mechanism without notes ten days from now. Which first action supplies the strongest new evidence for designing the next study step?
items.stimulus: The learner reread the explanation twice today and reports that every paragraph feels clear.

options.text: Reread the same explanation a third time and record how easy it feels.
Close the source, reconstruct the mechanism, and compare the attempt with a criterion-based answer model.
Copy the displayed summary into a new document with cleaner formatting.
Wait ten days without specifying any performance to attempt.

answer.summary: Attempt retrieval before review and judge the generated performance against declared criteria.
answer.reasoning: The closed-source reconstruction tests the future operation directly enough to reveal omissions. Comparison with an answer model then supports a targeted recovery action. Rereading or copying may support study, but neither supplies the same unaided performance evidence; waiting without a task creates a delay but no retrieval opportunity.
commonErrors: Selecting rereading because subjective ease is mistaken for evidence of later reconstruction.
Selecting waiting because spacing is mistaken for elapsed time without a scheduled retrieval operation.
Selecting copying because visible production is mistaken for generation from memory.

items.prompt: Design a twelve-day plan for a target currently classified as fragile. State the target performance, at least three retrieval opportunities including one changed-context task, the feedback and recovery step after each attempt, and a decision rule for adjusting the next opportunity. Explain why your sequence is evidence about durable performance rather than familiarity.
items.stimulus: The required final performance is to compare two already taught models and justify which model fits a new bounded case. The learner can name both models but currently confuses their selection conditions.

answer.correct: A complete response defines unaided model selection and justification as the target; schedules an initial retrieval, a delayed discrimination attempt, and a later changed-context selection task before day twelve; compares each attempt with criteria; prescribes targeted restudy and renewed retrieval after errors; and uses observed delayed performance to shorten, extend, support, or vary the next attempt. Exact days may differ when the reasoning is coherent and no universal interval is claimed.
answer.summary: Align every scheduled opportunity with the target operation, feedback, recovery, variation, and an adaptive decision rule.
answer.reasoning: The baseline is fragile because selection conditions are confused, so the first attempt should expose that distinction with support available after the attempt. Later opportunities should recur across time and require selection in changed cases. A calendar alone is incomplete: the plan becomes interpretable when each observation changes the next action. Repeated successful performance after delay and variation is stronger evidence than ease during repeated exposure, while still remaining a provisional judgment.
acceptableResponses: Any schedule with at least three separated retrieval opportunities before the target date, including changed-context model selection, provided the response states performance criteria, feedback, recovery, and an evidence-linked adjustment rule.

A plan that begins with immediate discrimination practice is acceptable for the already taught, confusable models in this stimulus; initially blocked practice followed by interleaving is also acceptable when a learner lacks a usable representation of either model.
commonErrors: Naming dates without specifying what the learner will generate or how it will be judged.
Placing answer review before every retrieval attempt.
Calling one immediate correct answer mastery.
Mixing models before the learner has a usable initial representation of each; this warning does not apply to the already taught, confusable models in this stimulus.
Claiming that the chosen day gaps are universally optimal.

items.prompt: Interpret the attempt record and prescribe the next retrieval opportunity. State what the record supports, what it does not support, and how you would change support, delay, or task form.
items.stimulus: Attempt 1, immediately after study: six of six criteria met with two prompts. Attempt 2, three days later: four of six criteria met without prompts; both missing criteria concern when the model does not apply. Attempt 3, four days later: six of six criteria met without prompts on the original case. The final assessment is seven days away and will use a new case.

answer.correct: The record supports improved unaided performance on the original case but not yet robust boundary discrimination in a new case. The next opportunity should occur before the final assessment and use a changed case that makes the two missing applicability criteria decisive. Feedback should compare the selection justification with those criteria; an error should trigger targeted recovery on the boundary followed by another changed-case retrieval, while success permits a later cumulative check closer to the required performance date.
answer.summary: Adapt the next task to the observed boundary error and the upcoming changed-case demand.
answer.reasoning: Prompted immediate success cannot establish unaided durability. The later original-case success is useful evidence, but it does not erase the prior boundary error or demonstrate transfer to the final format. Therefore task form must change, not merely repeat the original case. The seven-day horizon constrains when the next opportunity should occur, but it does not determine one uniquely correct day.
acceptableResponses: A response may choose any defensible day before the final assessment if it uses a changed case, targets the missing applicability conditions, includes criterion-based feedback, and states a recovery or expansion rule.
commonErrors: Repeating only the original case because it was successful.
Ignoring the two boundary criteria that failed.
Prescribing a day without specifying the retrieval operation and response to error.

items.prompt: Redesign the partners' preparation and follow-up. Your response must preserve individual retrieval before answer exchange, use the partners' artifacts as criterion-based feedback rather than material to copy, schedule a later changed-context attempt, and state how each performance pattern changes that learner's next step.
items.stimulus: Two laboratory partners must independently explain an already taught mechanism and select a control for an unfamiliar experimental result in an oral practical eleven days from now. They share twenty-five minutes today, then can exchange only one written message each tonight. Mira can state the steps but reverses one causal link; Leo reconstructs the sequence but omits the condition under which it does not apply. Their old routine was to annotate a completed solution together.

answer.correct: A strong redesign uses the shared twenty-five minutes for separate closed-source explanations or control selections before either partner sees the other's work. The partners then compare each artifact with declared causal-link and boundary-condition criteria and use their single written messages for target-specific feedback or a concise recovery cue, not a completed answer. Mira's reversed link triggers a focused model of that relation followed by an earlier renewed explanation; Leo's missing boundary triggers a contrasting experimental case in which the boundary decides the control. Each schedules an individual changed-result retrieval before day eleven. Accurate but case-bound performance triggers a varied result; accurate delayed performance permits a longer gap and cumulative check. The response explains why jointly annotating a completed solution does not supply equivalent individual evidence.
answer.summary: Adapt the retrieval loop to unequal errors and limited synchronous and asynchronous exchange without replacing individual generation with shared exposure.
answer.reasoning: The coordination constraints differ from the taught study-group case: there are two learners, one short meeting, one-message asynchronous feedback, distinct mechanism errors, and an oral practical that requires control selection. Separate retrieval preserves diagnostic evidence; criterion-based artifact comparison makes the limited exchange useful. Later individual retrieval is still required because a successful joint annotation or copied message can conceal who can explain the mechanism and select a control after delay.
acceptableResponses: Different allocations of the twenty-five minutes and two messages are acceptable if both partners retrieve before answer exchange, receive error-specific recovery, schedule a later changed-context task before the practical, and use a performance-linked decision rule.
commonErrors: Letting one partner's completed explanation or message replace the other's attempt.
Scheduling only today's meeting or message exchange with no later individual retrieval.
Giving both partners the same next step despite different causal-link and boundary errors.
Using the one-message limit for a completed answer rather than criterion-based feedback and a recovery cue.

criteria.description: Selects the closed-source retrieval attempt with criterion-based comparison.
Defines the required target operation and aligns at least three retrieval opportunities, including a changed context, with it.
Specifies criterion-based feedback, targeted recovery, and renewed retrieval after error.
Uses the baseline, retention horizon, delay, variation, and performance-linked adjustment rule without claiming one universal interval.

rubrics.title: Interpret and adapt an attempt record

criteria.description: Distinguishes what the sequence supports from the unsupported claim of general mastery.
Targets the observed boundary error with a changed-case retrieval and relevant criteria.
Links error and success patterns to specific recovery, support, variation, or spacing decisions.

rubrics.title: Transfer the loop to constrained partner coordination

criteria.description: Preserves individual generation before answer exchange and distinguishes retrieval from jointly annotating a completed solution.
Uses the limited partner exchange for criterion-based comparison and assigns recovery from each learner's specific evidence.
Schedules a later changed-context attempt and adapts each next step to the observed performance pattern.

sources.title: Evidence synthesis: writing university-grade learning content
sources.locator: Executive conclusion and limitations; sections 4-9 and 12; evidence-to-rule matrix. This rapid, decision-oriented synthesis is not a registered systematic review, and its included studies vary in learners, materials, comparison conditions, delays, feedback, and outcomes.

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Only bounded evidence conclusions and limitations are used as factual support; the lesson's organization, prose, examples, tables, prompts, and answer logic were independently created. Repository CONTENT-LICENSE.md identifies research prose as CC BY 4.0.

agentAccess.notes: Repository-owned source explicitly supplied for this author run. The repository content license includes research prose, and no access control or term prohibiting agent processing was found. This is a dated observation, not a source-owner warranty.

sources.title: EmbeddedKnowledge Learning-Content Standard v1
sources.locator: Project-internal authoring contract, sections 2-8 and 12-15. This is an EmbeddedKnowledge design convention authored by the same project and accountable principal, not an external standards-body publication or independent empirical source.

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Used only to identify project-internal design conventions and their stated limits; it is not treated as independent empirical evidence. No source wording, examples, question sequence, or expressive structure is copied. Repository CONTENT-LICENSE.md identifies CONTENT-STANDARD.md as CC BY 4.0.

agentAccess.notes: Repository-owned project contract explicitly required for this author run. The repository content license includes this file, and no access control or term prohibiting agent processing was found. Its internal origin is disclosed so it is not mistaken for external authority.

sources.title: Test-Enhanced Learning: Taking Memory Tests Improves Long-Term Retention
sources.locator: Psychological Science 17(3), 249-255; DOI 10.1111/j.1467-9280.2006.01693.x. Primary experiments with prose passages compared repeated study and retrieval at immediate and delayed tests; the narrow tasks and delays do not establish a universal effect for every learner or assessment.
sources.license: Publisher copyright; no open redistribution license confirmed in the Crossref record

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Used only for the general factual contrast between retrieval and repeated study across the reported delayed tests. No article prose, example, table, figure, or item is quoted, adapted, redistributed, or mirrored.

agentAccess.notes: The authoring agent inspected the PubMed abstract through NCBI E-utilities within the published request limits and verified the DOI and citation metadata through Crossref's public REST record. Neither route required login or bypassing access controls; publisher full text was not opened.

sources.title: Rethinking the Use of Tests: A Meta-Analysis of Practice Testing
sources.locator: Review of Educational Research 87(3), 659-701; DOI 10.3102/0034654316689306. Meta-analysis of practice testing across varied formats and conditions; heterogeneity in tests, feedback, learner populations, and final measures limits any single task prescription.
sources.license: Publisher copyright; no open redistribution license confirmed in the Crossref record

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Used only as synthesis-level factual support that retrieval practice has a positive average effect across heterogeneous educational comparisons. No source expression, examples, instruments, tables, figures, or datasets are reused.

agentAccess.notes: The authoring agent inspected the abstract and bibliographic metadata deposited in Crossref's public REST API and did not open publisher full text. Crossref states that its bibliographic metadata is publicly available and almost none is subject to copyright; no source expression was reused.

sources.title: Distributed Practice in Verbal Recall Tasks: A Review and Quantitative Synthesis
sources.locator: Psychological Bulletin 132(3), 354-380; DOI 10.1037/0033-2909.132.3.354. Quantitative synthesis of distributed verbal-recall practice, including the relation between spacing gap and desired retention interval; it does not specify one gap for all materials or retrieval plans.
sources.license: Publisher copyright; no open redistribution license displayed in the Crossref record

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Used only for factual support that distributed practice generally benefits later retention and that useful gaps depend partly on the retention interval. No wording, study materials, figures, tables, or data are reused.

agentAccess.notes: The authoring agent inspected the PubMed abstract through NCBI E-utilities within the published request limits and verified DOI metadata through Crossref. The publisher article was not opened; no login or access control was bypassed. The abstract itself distinguishes distributed learning episodes from a universal retrieval schedule.

sources.title: Similarity Matters: A Meta-Analysis of Interleaved Learning and Its Moderators
sources.locator: Psychological Bulletin 145(11), 1029-1052; DOI 10.1037/bul0000209. Meta-analysis of interleaved learning and moderators, including material similarity and task type; average effects do not make interleaving a universal replacement for supported acquisition.
sources.license: Publisher copyright; no open redistribution license displayed in the Crossref record

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Used only for the bounded factual claim that interleaving effects vary and are relevant to discrimination among confusable categories or strategies. No article expression, tasks, tables, figures, or data are reused.

agentAccess.notes: The authoring agent inspected the PubMed abstract through NCBI E-utilities within the published request limits and verified DOI metadata through Crossref. The abstract reports the material and task moderators recorded here. Publisher full text was not opened, and no access control was bypassed.

sources.title: The Power of Feedback Revisited: A Meta-Analysis of Educational Feedback Research
sources.locator: Frontiers in Psychology volume 10 (volume year 2019), article 3087; electronically published 2020-01-22; DOI 10.3389/fpsyg.2019.03087. Meta-analysis of educational feedback with substantial heterogeneity; it distinguishes information about task, process, and self-regulation from person-directed comments but does not prescribe one universal script or timing.

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Used only for general factual support about information-rich educational feedback and its heterogeneous effects. Although the article is CC BY 4.0, no article wording, table, figure, or intervention is copied or adapted.

agentAccess.notes: The authoring agent inspected the PubMed abstract through NCBI E-utilities within the published request limits and verified the DOI, date, publisher, and CC BY 4.0 record through Crossref. The abstract reports the heterogeneity and information-content moderation recorded here; no protected expression was reused.

sources.title: A Meta-Analysis of the Worked Examples Effect on Mathematics Performance
sources.publisher: Springer Science and Business Media LLC
sources.locator: Educational Psychology Review 35(1), article 11; DOI 10.1007/s10648-023-09745-1; ERIC EJ1364058. Meta-analysis of 55 studies and 181 effects on mathematics performance from elementary through postsecondary settings; it supports a worked-example default within that scope, not this lesson's exact planning sequence.
sources.license: Publisher copyright; no open redistribution license confirmed in the Crossref or ERIC metadata

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Used only for the factual claim that worked examples have a positive average effect in the reviewed mathematics settings. The lesson's fictional case, procedure, prompts, tables, and five-stage practice progression are independently authored and do not reproduce study materials.

agentAccess.notes: The public ERIC record and Crossref metadata were inspected for title, authors, date, DOI, study count, population range, and abstract-level limitations; publisher full text was not opened. No term prohibiting this limited public metadata access was found.

sources.title: A Cornerstone of Adaptivity: A Meta-Analysis of the Expertise Reversal Effect
sources.locator: Learning and Instruction 98, article 102142; DOI 10.1016/j.learninstruc.2025.102142. Meta-analysis of assistance-by-prior-knowledge interactions across 60 studies; effects vary by learner and domain and support adaptive assistance, not a fixed sequence for every learner.

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Used only for the general factual principle that the useful amount of instructional assistance depends on demonstrated prior knowledge. No source wording, framework, table, figure, or study material is copied or adapted.

agentAccess.notes: The authoring agent inspected the publicly accessible peDOCS citation and abstract record and verified the DOI, authors, date, journal, article number, and CC BY 4.0 license through Crossref. No login, access control, robots prohibition, or displayed term restricting this limited record access was found; publisher full text was not copied.

claims.statement: For this lesson's operational model, a mastery judgment should rely on observable unaided performance after delay and variation rather than on familiarity during exposure or one immediate success.
claims.scope: Operational planning model for foundation-level academic self-study and lesson sequencing, not a universal psychometric definition or professional competence standard.
claims.uncertainty: No single task, score, delay, or number of successful attempts establishes mastery across all learners, domains, and retention goals.

claims.statement: Across the educational research summarized by the repository, practice that requires retrieval can support more durable later retention than repeated exposure, although effects depend on task, feedback, and test conditions.
claims.scope: Academic learning tasks represented in the cited primary experiment, practice-testing meta-analysis, and repository rapid synthesis.
claims.uncertainty: Retrieval formats, learner knowledge, feedback, stakes, delays, and final assessments vary; the claim does not guarantee an effect for every task or learner.

claims.statement: Spreading retrieval across time generally benefits later retention, while a useful gap depends partly on how long the learner needs to retain and use the knowledge; the evidence does not yield one universal schedule.
claims.scope: Planning academic retrieval across multiple opportunities and a stated retention horizon.
claims.uncertainty: The quantitative synthesis concerns spaced learning episodes generally; applying it to spaced retrieval is a bounded planning choice. The best gap varies with retention interval, material, prior knowledge, retrievability, and the outcome being assessed.

claims.statement: After a genuine retrieval attempt, feedback is most useful for this design loop when it identifies correctness, explains the relevant principle or error, and directs a specific recovery action.
claims.scope: Formative feedback in the academic retrieval-and-recovery cycle taught in this lesson.
claims.uncertainty: Feedback interventions are heterogeneous; timing and detail should match the task and learner rather than follow one fixed script.

claims.statement: Interleaving is a conditional design choice that is especially relevant when learners must discriminate among confusable, already introduced categories or strategies, rather than a universal replacement for supported initial acquisition.
claims.scope: Selection and discrimination practice after learners have a usable initial representation of each category or strategy.
claims.uncertainty: Average interleaving effects vary by material similarity, task type, learner preparation, and comparison condition.

claims.statement: During initial acquisition of a new procedure, a complete worked model can reduce demands on novice learners; as learners demonstrate accurate performance, support can be adjusted so they perform more of the procedure unaided.
claims.scope: Foundation-level learners acquiring a new procedure, interpreted alongside evidence from mathematics and other structured learning domains.
claims.uncertainty: The permitted evidence supports worked examples in mathematics and performance-sensitive assistance across heterogeneous learning settings, not this project's exact five-stage progression; effects vary with prior knowledge, domain, task, and the form of assistance.

terms.definition: In this lesson, a provisional judgment supported by a pattern of successful unaided performance after delay and variation under declared conditions; it is not a universal threshold or a professional credential.
Observable reconstruction or use of knowledge after a meaningful delay, under stated conditions, rather than only during the original study encounter.
A subjective sense that material is recognizable or easy while it is present; by itself, it does not show that the learner can reconstruct or use the material later.
An attempt to generate a relevant answer, explanation, prediction, representation, or procedure from memory before consulting the answer source.
Distributing learning or retrieval opportunities across time instead of placing all opportunities in one uninterrupted session.

The whole future period over which the learner needs to retain and use the target knowledge, used here to constrain a retrieval plan. This planning horizon is not the technical retention interval, which is the time between the last practice opportunity and a later test.
Information after an attempt that identifies what was correct or incorrect, explains the relevant principle or process, and directs a useful next action.
Mixing already introduced categories or strategies so that the learner must select and discriminate among them rather than repeat one type in a block.
A predeclared link between observed performance and the next planning action, such as adding support after a missing relation or extending a gap after successful delayed performance.

attributionHtml:
Attribution and provenance

Lesson content license

Original lesson content in PREM-LPP-003, version 0.1.4, is contributed by github:VSBDev under the Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International license (CC BY 4.0).

Suggested attribution: Based on EmbeddedKnowledge, “PREM-LPP-003 — Design retrieval and spacing for durable performance,” version 0.1.4, by VSBDev, licensed CC BY 4.0. Changes: describe any changes made.

Accountable authorship and agent disclosure

  • Accountable principal: github:VSBDev.
  • Material authoring agent: Codex CLI.
  • Provider: OpenAI.
  • Model: GPT-5.
  • Agent version: Codex CLI 0.144.6.
  • Isolated author run ID: /root/author-loop.
  • Current material-instructions digest: sha256:f2122a6d54ed92adcf12ee5436c7be69107499f2276adf9cecebeed6f23204f9.
  • Digest scope: the exact discloseable version-0.1.4 author-revision assignment supplied to this run, including the source-access removal, independent evidence reauthoring, academic terminology and scoring clarifications, learning-feedback exemplar, provenance, validation, and freeze requirements; hidden provider and platform instructions are excluded.
  • Original version-0.1.0 authoring digest: sha256:ca27adccf1f8c30a8c9c956ea0eec64c6c70c505af6a51e4603f1ad12fed708b.
  • Version-0.1.1 revision digest: sha256:fc27660f45d322d9c6f494dcfdc24b664db520f373dff3f55242f4672b86ccf4.
  • Version-0.1.2 revision digest: sha256:1cd0892acd20e5d87b2a85caa6ad2e0aed70825af1adee2e31eff3ac607aeb92.
  • Version-0.1.3 revision digest: sha256:54476e53117303fd601e50af4091418846cb7040df00a316662ea48c4a8e66d3.

This record discloses and attests the provenance supplied by the accountable principal. It is not an independent verification of provider, model, or run identity. No review or adjudication role was performed in this run.

Version 0.1.4 uses the preserved academic and learning-design approvals and accessibility-and-rights request-changes review of candidate 54f10aa5d4e3d0d226426dd3a70b611ad0572dc1 only as external revision feedback. The exact historical artifacts remain under reviews/, and their artifact IDs, GitHub transport IDs, target commit, and stale-review boundary are recorded in REVISION-HISTORY.md. They do not review or approve version 0.1.4.

Sources and reuse

The lesson retains the repository-owned rapid synthesis as a consensus map for claims where it remains mapped and identifies source-content-standard truthfully as a project-internal authoring contract rather than an external standard. Permitted external records provide direct traceability for retrieval, spacing, interleaving, feedback, worked examples, and assistance-by-prior-knowledge effects. The guidance-fading claim is independently synthesized from the worked-example and expertise-reversal records and does not use the removed Crissman record or the repository synthesis as its evidence. Each retained record identifies its DOI or durable identifier, bibliographic metadata, use, limitations, displayed license state, rights basis, and the limited agent-access route used for verification.

The external publications are used only for facts. Publisher full text was not required for DOI metadata checks except where a public abstract record was inspected, and no source expression, organization, study item, example, table, figure, or dataset is reused. The lesson's structure, fictional cases, planning tables, questions, assessment items, feedback, and wording were independently created. The evidence supports general principles under heterogeneous conditions; it does not validate this lesson's exact five-stage practice progression, schedules, decision rules, or local mastery threshold.

No quotation, close paraphrase, adapted expression, redistributed media, or dataset extraction is used. No third-party asset is included. The planning tables and fictional cases were created for this lesson and contain no personal, learner, or patient data.

Boundaries

This lesson provides academic study-planning instruction. It does not establish university credit, admissions readiness, clinical competence, a medical diagnosis, or treatment advice. Any mastery judgment produced by the lesson is provisional and bounded by the declared task, delay, and conditions.
